text: The surgeon uses the lithotomy position and fan-belly tract for retraction, elevating the gallbladder upwards. A small hemangioma on the right side is excised separately.
The surgeon skeletonizes the hilum of the left pedicle, carefully exposing the vascular and biliary structures within the liver hilum.
The surgeon clips and divides the cystic artery during the laparoscopic liver resection procedure.
The surgeon controls the left hepatic artery by carefully dissecting around it and placing a ligature to secure the vessel.
The surgeon places a ligature around the hemangioma, causing it to shrink slightly. After double clipping, the hemangioma is divided, and the dissection continues.
The surgeon visualizes the left portal vein deeper within the hilum, following the previous skeletonization of the pedicle on the left side.
The surgeon performs circumferential dissection around the hilum, sealing and dividing smaller parenchymal communications.
The surgeon doubly ligates the left portal vein, securing it with ligatures to prevent blood flow.
The surgeon begins mobilizing the left lobe of the liver by dividing the falciform ligament, progressing towards the hepatic islets.
The surgeon observes the regression in size of the hemangioma and proceeds to divide the falciform ligament on both sides.
The surgeon clips the hepatic duct and divides it, completing the dissection around the liver hilum.
The surgeon divides the cystic duct and separates the gallbladder. A stitch is placed at the right side of segment V of the liver, preparing for the division of the parenchymal capillaries.
The surgeon begins resection at the principal plane of the liver, using a harmonic scalpel to mark the extended margin up to the cranial division.
The surgeon clips and divides the identifiable vessels and ducts within the surgical field, ensuring clear separation from surrounding structures.
The surgeon continues cranial dissection, identifying the left hepatic vein. A bleeding vein is observed, initially clipped and then sutured to control the bleeding.
The surgical team mobilizes the left triangular ligament, detaching it completely from the left lobe of the liver up to the origin of the left hepatic vein.
The surgeon uses a stapler to divide the vein across the liver parenchyma.
The segment of the liver is completely separated, and the hepatic duct is positioned on the left side.
In the magnified view, the surgeon clearly visualizes the ducts, which are either clipped or sutured to ensure proper closure, aiming to enhance postoperative recovery and reduce morbidity.
The surgeon uses organoplasma coagulation for hemostasis, followed by a final inspection of the surgical area. The gallbladder is visibly separated from the liver.
